content:
# Mark Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 33r–33v (PDF pages 65–66) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_004_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_004_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Once again Jesus began teaching by the lake. The crowd that gathered around him was so enormous that he climbed into a boat and sat in it on the water, while the entire crowd stood along the shore.

² He taught them many things through stories, and in his teaching he said:

³ "Listen! A farmer went out to plant his seed.

⁴ As he scattered it, some fell on the footpath, and the birds came and ate it up.

⁵ Some fell on shallow soil over rock. It sprouted quickly because the soil was thin,

⁶ but when the sun beat down, it was scorched, and since it had no deep roots, it dried up and died.

⁷ Other seed fell among thorn bushes, which grew up and choked it out, so it produced no crop.

⁸ But some seed fell on rich soil. It came up, grew tall, and produced a harvest—thirty, sixty, even a hundred times what was planted."

⁹ Then he said, "If you have ears, then hear!"

¹⁰ When the crowd had gone and he was alone with the twelve and the others around him, they asked him what the stories meant.

¹¹ He told them, "You have been let in on the secret of God's kingdom. But for those on the outside, everything remains in stories,

¹² so that, as Isaiah said, 'They may look and look but never see; they may listen and listen but never understand—otherwise they might turn and be forgiven.'"

¹³ Then he said, "You don't understand this story? Then how will you understand any of my stories?

¹⁴ Here's what it means. The farmer plants the message.

¹⁵ The seed that fell on the path represents those who hear the message, but Satan immediately comes and snatches it away from them.

¹⁶ The seed on rocky soil represents those who hear the message and instantly embrace it with excitement.

¹⁷ But they have no staying power—they're rootless. When trouble or persecution comes because of the message, they quickly give up.

¹⁸ The seed among thorns represents others who hear the message,

¹⁹ but then the worries of everyday life, the seduction of wealth, and the craving for all sorts of things crowd in and strangle the message, leaving it barren.

²⁰ But the seed planted in rich soil represents those who hear the message, take it in, and produce a harvest—thirty, sixty, even a hundred times over."

²¹ He continued, "Would you light a lamp and then put it under a bucket or shove it under the bed? Of course not—you put it on a stand where it can give light.

²² Everything that is hidden will eventually be brought out into the open. Every secret will come to light.

²³ If you have ears, then hear!

²⁴ Think carefully about what you hear," he said. "The standard you use will be the standard used for you—and then some.

²⁵ Those who have will be given more. Those who have nothing—even what little they have will be taken from them."

²⁶ He also said, "Here is what God's kingdom is like. A man scatters seed on the ground.

²⁷ Day after day he sleeps and gets up, and the seed sprouts and grows—though he has no idea how it happens.

²⁸ The soil does the work all by itself: first the green shoot, then the head of grain, then the ripe wheat in the head.

²⁹ And when the crop is ready, the man swings the sickle, because harvest time has come."

³⁰ He went on: "What picture can we use for God's kingdom? What story will capture it?

³¹ It's like a mustard seed. When you plant it in the ground, it's the tiniest seed you can imagine.

³² But once it's planted, it shoots up and becomes the biggest plant in the garden, with branches so large that birds come and nest in its shade."

³³ Jesus used many stories like these to share the message with the crowds—as much as they could take in.

³⁴ He never taught them without using stories. But when he was alone with his disciples, he explained the full meaning of everything.

³⁵ That evening he said to his disciples, "Let's cross over to the other side of the lake."

³⁶ They left the crowd behind and set out with Jesus in the boat, just as he was. Several other boats went along.

³⁷ Suddenly a ferocious windstorm swept in. Waves crashed over the sides of the boat, and it began to fill with water.

³⁸ Jesus was at the back of the boat, sound asleep on a cushion. They shook him awake, shouting, "Teacher! Don't you care that we're about to drown?"

³⁹ He got up, spoke sharply to the wind, and commanded the sea, "Quiet! Be still!" The wind dropped dead, and everything went perfectly calm.

⁴⁰ Then he turned to them: "Why are you such cowards? Do you still have no faith?"

⁴¹ They were overcome with awe—terrified, really—and whispered to each other, "Who is this man? Even the wind and the waves do what he says!"

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **"Stories" for "parables":** I render παραβολαί as "stories" because the English word "parable" has become a dead religious term. Jesus was telling vivid, earthy stories drawn from everyday agricultural life. A farmer. Seed. Dirt. Thorns. These are not abstract theological propositions.

2. **v. 12 — the hardest saying in the chapter:** Jesus quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 to explain why he teaches in parables—and his answer is disturbing: "so that" they won't understand. Mark's ΙΝΑ is purposive, not merely resultative. I preserve this scandal rather than softening it.

3. **"The wind dropped dead" (v. 39):** The Greek says "the wind ceased" (ἐκόπασεν), but placed immediately after Jesus's commanding rebuke—the same verb he used to silence a demon in chapter 1—the effect is stunning and instantaneous. I captured that abruptness.

4. **"Such cowards" (v. 40):** The Greek δειλοί is not polite concern. It's blunt—cowardly, spineless. Jesus has just saved their lives, and his first response is to challenge their faith. I preserve the sting.

5. **"Sound asleep on a cushion" (v. 38):** This is one of the most human details in the Gospels—Jesus so exhausted that he sleeps through a life-threatening storm on a boat cushion. Mark's eyewitness details (often attributed to Peter's testimony) give us moments like this that no theologian would invent.

6. **The parable of the growing seed (vv. 26-29):** This parable is unique to Mark—found nowhere else. Its central point: the kingdom grows by its own mysterious power, not by human effort. The word αὐτομάτη ("all by itself") is the theological heart of the passage.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
